@breznak This will break the future builds!

```
#if defined(NTA_PLATFORM_win32) && defined(NTA_COMPILER_MSVC)
...
#elif defined(NTA_ARCH_32)
...
#endif
```

If by any of the conditions in `#if defined(NTA_PLATFORM_win32) && defined(NTA_COMPILER_MSVC)` is false, the compiler will can process non-Windows ASM in `#elif defined(NTA_ARCH_32)`!

Maybe you should use:
`#elif !defined(NTA_PLATFORM_win32) && defined(NTA_ARCH_32)`
defined(NTA_PLATFORM_win32)

I was trying to avoid a `Vec<Vec<u8>>` because I don't want to keep the (potentially large) file being uploaded in memory twice (once for the original `Vec<u8>`, once for the `Vec<Vec<u8>>`), as well as 2 copies of the N chunks currently in flight in both the unserializezd proto and the serialized proto.

If I do `future::stream::iter_ok::<_, grpcio::Error>(bytes.chunks(self.chunk_size_bytes))`, I get complaints that `bytes` doesn't live long enough, because nothing takes ownership of it, so it gets dropped at the end of `store_bytes`. I tried writing a custom `Iterator` implementation which holds on to the underlying `Vec`, but couldn't make it work with as much work as I could justify.

But this is a good catch - I did a little benchmarking, and the difference is massive; chunking before putting into a stream is about 15% faster. I switched to do an eager copy of the chunks, but would love some help somehow passing ownership of the original `Vec` so that the chunking can happen lazily.

Indent 4 spaces (this is how sphinx works out that it is processing a new block). In fact for any new directive you have to have a new line before the directive, a line after the directive, and then the content of the directive block must be indented by 4 spaces. So for example a note would look like this:

```
some text.

.. note::

    The note content.
    This is more note content.
```

If the purpose of this line is to split out the extension of the filename then using strip can be dangerous. Consider the following example.

```python
>>> '.gg.asjkdhajkh.zz.gz'.strip('.gz')
'asjkdhajkh'
```
strip will remove any combination of the given arg characters at either end. The proper way to achieve what I think that you want is to use `os.path.splitext` as follows:

```python
>>> os.path.splitext('.gg.asjkdhajkh.zz.gz')[0]
'.gg.asjkdhajkh.zz'
```

In base class we have `ThreadError Mle::SendChildUpdateRequest(void)` and in its sub-class `MleRouter` we have `ThreadError MleRouter::SendChildUpdateRequest(Child *aChild)` which then hides the base class one.
